video_title: Private Markets at an Inflection Point
gist: Goldman's Pete Lyon and Mike Brandmeyer argue private markets are at a structural inflection point: the PE distribution 'circulatory system' is gummed up (holds at ~7 years, distributions at 8-10% of NAV vs ~20% normal) but poised to normalize over 1-3 years absent the Iran War. They defend private credit as resilient (defaults ~2% vs >10% in past cycles), see PE alpha as cyclical (recently negative vs ripping public markets), and flag secondaries, retail democratization, and the innovation economy staying private as durable growth.

insights.claim: Goldman frames alternatives as undergoing a market-structure change analogous to the decimalization and ETF-driven liquidity transformation of public equities, implying private assets are becoming more tradable and liquid over time.
insights.speaker: Pete Lyon, global co-head Capital Solutions Group, Goldman Sachs
insights.so_what: A structural liquidity transformation in alts reshapes fee models, secondary pricing, and access — a multi-year theme for asset managers.
insights.quote: we're in the middle of a market structure change

insights.claim: Private markets grew roughly six times between 2010 (post-GFC) and 2022, then froze when the Fed hiked rates ~500bps, marking the start of the current four-year inflection.
insights.speaker: Mike Brandmeyer, global head & CIO External Investing Group, GSAM
insights.so_what: Quantifies the boom-then-freeze that set up today's liquidity logjam; the unfreeze is the catalyst to watch.

insights.claim: The private-markets industry is consolidating into a barbell: large multi-line public asset managers on one end, discrete alpha-driven specialist strategies on the other, with mid-sized firms facing an existential funding-model question.
insights.speaker: Pete Lyon
insights.so_what: Mid-tier GPs are the squeeze point — M&A and consolidation targets; favor either scale players or top-decile specialists.
insights.quote: What you're seeing is a very barbell-like structure

insights.claim: PE distributions have been stuck at ~8-10% of NAV over the last three years versus a historical norm of ~20%, because Fed rate hikes made levered assets worth less while marks were slow to come down, freezing the 'circulatory system.'
insights.speaker: Mike Brandmeyer
insights.so_what: Distribution rate is the key normalization metric; a return toward 15-20% would re-open the fundraising and re-up cycle.

insights.claim: Average buyout hold periods have risen to almost seven years (from ~5.5 years a decade ago) and the average venture company now takes 14 years to IPO, lengthening capital lockup and gumming up fundraising.
insights.speaker: Mike Brandmeyer
insights.so_what: Longer holds delay LP cash returns and pressure GP fundraising — favors secondaries and NAV-lending solutions providers.

insights.claim: Goldman expects distributions and deal activity to normalize over 1-3 years and potentially exceed the 2021 peak within two to three years, driven by economic growth lifting operating earnings, easing rates, IPO backlog growth, and LP pressure on GPs.
insights.speaker: Mike Brandmeyer
insights.so_what: A bullish 2-3 year setup for PE monetization and deal flow — constructive for sponsors, advisors, and IPO pipeline names.
insights.quote: you could be seeing distribution environments and deal activities that are even above what you saw in 2021

insights.claim: But for the Iran War, Goldman believed 2026 was setting up to be a great year for distributions, with a considerable mandate pipeline and a growing IPO backlog — making geopolitics the swing variable on the timeline.
insights.speaker: Mike Brandmeyer
insights.so_what: A Middle East de-escalation/offramp is the near-term catalyst that could unlock the distribution wave in 2026.

insights.claim: Sponsors are not walking away but using an expanding liquidity spectrum — NAV-based lending, GP cash-flow lending, secondaries, and structured transactions — beyond traditional IPOs and M&A to engineer monetizations.
insights.speaker: Pete Lyon
insights.so_what: Growth in hybrid/structured liquidity tools is a durable revenue stream for capital-solutions providers; don't underestimate sponsor creativity.
insights.quote: the world is awash with liquidity right now

themes.theme: Private credit resilience vs. press narrative

insights.claim: Private credit default rates are at ~2% or below today versus over 10% in prior cycles like the GFC, and Goldman sees no systemic credit problem despite negative press.
insights.speaker: Pete Lyon
insights.so_what: Contrarian to the 'cracks in private credit' narrative; argues current weakness is idiosyncratic, not systemic.

insights.claim: Even in a stressed scenario with a 10% default rate and 50%+ of private credit underperforming, losses would only be ~5%, versus a 50%+ peak-to-trough drawdown in equities over the same GFC period.
insights.speaker: Pete Lyon
insights.so_what: Quantifies private credit's downside asymmetry — a resilience argument for allocating through a credit cycle.

insights.claim: Pockets of private credit weakness are concentrated in software-centric companies and businesses left overlevered from the 2021/2022 LBO period, not the asset class broadly.
insights.speaker: Pete Lyon
insights.so_what: Targets where to underweight within private credit — vintage-2021/22 and software-heavy exposures are the soft spots.

insights.claim: A burgeoning private-credit trading/secondary market is emerging, raising the prospect that private credit converges toward public credit in liquidity over time.
insights.speaker: Pete Lyon
insights.so_what: A tradable private-credit market would compress the public/private distinction and reprice illiquidity premia.

themes.theme: PE vs. public markets and cyclical alpha

insights.claim: PE three-year rolling returns went negative in alpha versus public markets for the first time over the last two years, because public markets rose 40-50% while smoothed PE marks lagged.
insights.speaker: Mike Brandmeyer
insights.so_what: Explains the LP question of whether the illiquidity premium still exists; recent PE underperformance is mark-timing, not value destruction.

insights.claim: PE alpha is cyclical and pro-cyclical (because it's levered): roughly flat when public markets are up >10%, strong alpha in 0-10% environments, and dramatic outperformance when public markets are negative.
insights.speaker: Mike Brandmeyer
insights.so_what: A timing framework — PE shines most as a public-market drawdown hedge; expect mean-reversion of relative performance.

insights.claim: Historical illiquidity premiums reached as high as 1200 bps versus public markets but have compressed lately; Goldman/academics view 100-150 bps as still worthwhile for a diversified portfolio.
insights.speaker: Pete Lyon / Mike Brandmeyer
insights.so_what: Sets a realistic bar for the durable illiquidity premium (~1-1.5 pts) after compression — informs PE allocation expectations.

insights.claim: Public-market outperformance is highly concentrated/dispersed, whereas a diversified PE portfolio captures broader outperformance over a longer hold — an argument for PE's role despite recent public-market strength.
insights.speaker: Pete Lyon
insights.so_what: Frames PE as a diversification play against narrow public-market leadership concentration.

insights.claim: Most new-company innovation will live in private markets long-term because firms can now raise tens of billions privately, removing the need to IPO — contrasted with Amazon going public in 1997 just to raise $54 million.
insights.speaker: Mike Brandmeyer
insights.so_what: The innovation economy staying private structurally shifts return access away from public-market investors toward private allocators.

insights.claim: The PE secondary market was about $250 billion last year and Goldman estimates it could reach ~$500 billion within three to five years, roughly doubling as a liquidity escape valve.
insights.speaker: Mike Brandmeyer
insights.so_what: A doubling secondaries market is a concrete growth runway for secondary funds and intermediaries.

insights.claim: Retail participation in private credit has compounded ~60% annually over the last five-six years and now makes up ~20% of the private credit market, which exposed a liquidity mismatch when retail investors sought redemptions.
insights.speaker: Pete Lyon
insights.so_what: Rapid retail penetration plus illiquid structures is the real fragility — watch redemption gates and evergreen-fund liquidity provisions.

insights.claim: Goldman is bullish on democratizing retail access to alternatives over the next decade-plus but insists on guardrails and education, including working with other firms in the evergreen space.
insights.speaker: Mike Brandmeyer
insights.so_what: Retail democratization is a structural AUM-growth tailwind for alts managers building evergreen/wealth products.
